nouns.articleHelp: Short/monosyllabic noun вЂ” no suffix or semantic rule applies. Short neuter nouns are less common (~25% of monosyllables). Many short das-nouns form -er plurals (Buchв†’BГјcher, Kindв†’Kinder, Hausв†’HГ¤user). Memorize in context.
nouns.pluralHelp: -e (die Jahre). Rule: Some neuter nouns take -e, usually WITHOUT umlaut: Jahrв†’Jahre, Spielв†’Spiele, Zielв†’Ziele, Tierв†’Tiere. Neuter -e plurals rarely umlaut (exception: das FloГџв†’FlГ¶Гџe).

nouns.articleHelp: Semantic note: das Kind вЂ” neuter despite referring to a person. This is a famous case where grammatical gender в‰  natural gender. Same for das Baby, das MГ¤dchen (-chen diminutive в†’ always das).
nouns.pluralHelp: -er (die Kinder). Rule: The -er plural is the signature neuter pattern for short nouns. E.g. Kindв†’Kinder, Buchв†’BГјcher, Hausв†’HГ¤user, Bildв†’Bilder. Almost always adds umlaut if possible.

nouns.articleHelp: вљ  Weak noun (n-Deklination): This noun takes -(e)n in ALL cases except nominative singular. den Menschen, dem Menschen, des Menschen. Recognizing weak nouns is crucial for correct case usage beyond just the article.
nouns.pluralHelp: -(e)n (die Menschen). вљ  Weak noun (n-Deklination): This noun takes -(e)n not just in plural, but in ALL cases except nominative singular. den Menschen, dem Menschen, des Menschen. The plural form is the same as the accusative/dative/genitive singular. Weak nouns must be memorized as a group.

nouns.articleHelp: Morphological: Nominalized infinitive в†’ always das. Any German verb can be used as a noun (capitalize it), and it's always neuter. das Leben (to live в†’ life), das Essen (to eat в†’ food).
nouns.pluralHelp: No change (die Unternehmen). Rule: Masculine and neuter nouns ending in -er, -el, -en usually keep the same plural form. Some add an umlaut (Vaterв†’VГ¤ter), but many don't (Fensterв†’Fenster). The article change to 'die' is your only plural signal.

nouns.pluralHelp: -(e)n (die Zeiten). Rule: The most common feminine plural. Nearly all die-nouns that don't end in -e take -(e)n: Frauв†’Frauen, TГјrв†’TГјren, Uhrв†’Uhren, Zahlв†’Zahlen. When you know it's die, guess -(e)n for the plural and you'll usually be right.

nouns.articleHelp: Short/monosyllabic noun вЂ” no suffix or semantic rule applies. Statistically, ~60% of monosyllabic German nouns are der, ~25% das, ~15% die. But that's a gamble, not a rule. Best memorized in a phrase: 'der Hund bellt', 'der Weg ist lang'.
